questions.text: Why does the Surah swear by the Day of Resurrection and the self-reproaching soul?
questions.answer: To highlight the reality of judgment and the inner conscience that knows right from wrong.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah teaches that conscience is useless and has no spiritual value.
questions.answer: False. The self-reproaching soul is presented as meaningful and connected to accountability.

questions.text: What does mentioning fingertips teach about resurrection?
questions.answer: Allah can recreate humans with perfect detail, so resurrection is not difficult for Him.

questions.text: What does the Surah teach about the psychological reason people deny the Hereafter?
questions.answer: Some want to keep sinning without consequences, so they deny what would restrain them.

questions.text: How does the Surah describe the cosmic changes on the Day of Judgment?
questions.answer: It describes dramatic changes like the moon’s eclipse and the collapse of familiar order.

questions.text: What does the Surah teach about trying to escape on that Day?
questions.answer: There is no refuge; people will realize escape is impossible.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah teaches that people can hide behind excuses successfully on Judgment Day.
questions.answer: False. The human being will witness against himself, even with excuses.

questions.text: What does it mean that a person’s own self will testify against them?
questions.answer: Their conscience and reality of deeds will expose them; self-deception will collapse.

questions.text: What does Surah Al-Qiyamah teach about the Prophet’s behavior during revelation?
questions.answer: He was taught not to rush recitation; Allah ensured its collection and recitation.

questions.text: What is one lesson about trusting Allah from the revelation instruction?
questions.answer: Trust Allah’s process; do your part without panic or impatience.

questions.text: How does the Surah explain why some people prefer dunya over akhirah?
questions.answer: They love the immediate and neglect the heavier, future reality.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah teaches that love of dunya is harmless as long as a person is polite.
questions.answer: False. Loving dunya over akhirah leads to denial and loss.

questions.text: What does the Surah teach about the faces of believers on that Day?
questions.answer: They are radiant and honored, reflecting safety and hope.

questions.text: What does the Surah teach about the faces of disbelievers on that Day?
questions.answer: They are gloomy and fearful, expecting a crushing calamity.

questions.text: How does the Surah describe the moment of death as a turning point?
questions.answer: People realize separation is happening; worldly control disappears and truth becomes near.

questions.text: What is one daily-life lesson from reflecting on the moment of death?
questions.answer: Do not postpone repentance; prepare while you can still act.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah teaches that prayer is optional as long as belief is present.
questions.answer: False. It mentions abandoning prayer as a sign of rejection and wrongdoing.

questions.text: What does the Surah teach about people who deny truth and turn away?
questions.answer: Their end is blameworthy; denial is paired with moral failure and arrogance.

questions.text: What does the repeated warning 'woe to you' teach about ignoring reminders?
questions.answer: That denial is a dangerous path with escalating consequences.

questions.text: How does Surah Al-Qiyamah teach that life is purposeful?
questions.answer: It rejects the idea of being left neglected; creation implies accountability and return.

questions.text: What does the Surah teach about Allah’s ability to give life to the dead?
questions.answer: It ends by affirming that the One who created can resurrect—this is fully within Allah’s power.

questions.text: What is one way to strengthen the self-reproaching soul in a healthy way?
questions.answer: Use conscience to repent and improve, not to despair or self-hate.

questions.text: True/False: The Surah teaches that guilt should lead to hopelessness.
questions.answer: False. The Surah’s warnings are meant to lead to repentance and reform.

questions.text: How can this Surah help someone struggling with self-deception?
questions.answer: It exposes the reality of accountability and makes excuses feel useless, encouraging honest repentance.

questions.text: How can a modern reader apply this Surah without memorizing verses?
questions.answer: Use its themes—death, record, conscience—to do daily self-audits and correct one habit.

questions.text: What does Allah swear by in the first two verses?
questions.answer: The Day of Resurrection and the 'self-reproaching soul' (an-Nafs al-Lawwamah).

questions.text: What part of the human body does Allah say He is able to perfectly reconstruct?
questions.answer: The fingertips (proportion of the fingers).

questions.text: What does the Surah describe as a desire of the disbeliever?
questions.answer: To continue sinning ahead of him (denying what is to come).

questions.text: What will happen to the moon and the sun on that Day?
questions.answer: The moon will be eclipsed, and the sun and moon will be joined together.

questions.text: Where will man try to flee on the Day of Judgment?
questions.answer: He will ask: 'Where is the escape?' but there is no refuge.

questions.text: Who will be a witness against the human being on that Day?
questions.answer: The human himself; his own self will be a witness against him even if he presents excuses.

questions.text: What was the Prophet (SAW) commanded NOT to do during the revelation of the Quran?
questions.answer: Not to move his tongue quickly to hasten its recitation/memorization.

questions.text: Who is responsible for the collection and recitation of the Quran?
questions.answer: Allah.

questions.text: Why do disbelievers reject the Hereafter according to this Surah’s message?
questions.answer: Because they love the immediate (world) and leave the Hereafter.

questions.text: How will the faces of the believers look on that Day?
questions.answer: Radiant (Nadirah), looking toward their Lord.

questions.text: How will the faces of the disbelievers look on that Day?
questions.answer: Contorted/gloomy (Basirah), expecting a back-breaking calamity.

questions.text: What is the state of the soul when it reaches the collarbones (death)?
questions.answer: It will be asked: 'Who is a healer?' and the dying person will realize it is the parting.

questions.text: How are the legs described at the moment of death?
questions.answer: The leg will be wound about the [other] leg.

questions.text: What does the Surah mention the disbeliever failed to do?
questions.answer: He did not believe, he did not pray, and he denied the truth and turned away.

questions.text: What is the repeated warning given to the disbeliever in this Surah?
questions.answer: 'Woe to you, and woe! Then woe to you, and woe!'.

questions.text: Does man think he will be left 'neglected' or 'without purpose'?
questions.answer: No.

questions.text: What are the stages of creation mentioned at the end of the Surah?
questions.answer: A sperm-drop, then a clinging clot, then Allah fashioned and proportioned him.

questions.text: What did Allah make from the human creation?
questions.answer: The two mates, the male and the female.

questions.text: What is the final rhetorical question of the Surah?
questions.answer: 'Is not That One able to give life to the dead?'.

questions.text: What is 'An-Nafs al-Lawwamah'?
questions.answer: The self-reproaching soul that blames itself for its shortcomings, which is a sign of faith.
